coredata.dc:title: An overview of nanomaterial-based novel disinfection technologies for harmful microorganisms: Mechanism, synthesis, devices and application
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zhong-Ting
Chen, Yue
Fei, Yan-Fei
Loo, Siew-Leng
Chen, Guancong
Hu, Mian
Song, Yujie
Zhao, Jun
Zhang, Yifeng
Wang, Jiade
coredata.dc:description: Harmful microorganism (e.g., new coronavirus) based infection is the most important security concern in life sciences and healthcare. This article aims to provide a state-of-the-art review on the development of advanced technology based on nanomaterial disinfection/sterilization techniques (NDST) for the first time including the nanomaterial types, disinfection techniques, bactericidal devices, sterilization products, and application scenarios (i.e., water, air, medical healthcare), with particular brief account of bactericidal behaviors referring to varied systems. In this emerging research area spanning the years from 1998 to 2021, total of ~200 publications selected for the type of review paper and research articles were reviewed. Four typical functional materials (namely type of metal/metal oxides, S-based, C-based, and N-based) with their development progresses in disinfection/sterilization are summarized with a list of synthesis and design. Among them, the widely used silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are considered as the most effective bacterial agents in the type of nanomaterials at present and has been reported for inactivation of viruses, fungi, protozoa. Some methodologies against (1) disinfection by-products (DBPs) in traditional sterilization, (2) noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) agglomeration and release, (3) toxic metal leaching, (4) solar spectral response broadening, and (5) photogenerated e−/h+ pairs recombination are reviewed and discussed in this field, namely (1) alternative techniques and nanomaterials, (2) supporter anchoring effect, (3) nonmetal functional nanomaterials, (4) element doping, and (5) heterojunction constructing. The feasible strategies in the perspective of NDST are proposed to involve (1) non-noble metal disinfectors, (2) multi-functional nanomaterials, (3) multi-component nanocomposite innovation, and (4) hybrid techniques for disinfection/sterilization system. It is promising to achieve 100% bactericidal efficiency for 108 CFU/mL within a short time of less than 30 min.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Harmful microorganism (e.g., new coronavirus) based infection is the most important security concern in life sciences and healthcare. This article aims to provide a state-of-the-art review on the development of advanced technology based on nanomaterial disinfection/sterilization techniques (NDST) for the first time including the nanomaterial types, disinfection techniques, bactericidal devices, sterilization products, and application scenarios (i.e., water, air, medical healthcare), with particular brief account of bactericidal behaviors referring to varied systems. In this emerging research area spanning the years from 1998 to 2021, total of ~200 publications selected for the type of review paper and research articles were reviewed. Four typical functional materials (namely type of metal/metal oxides, S-based, C-based, and N-based) with their development progresses in disinfection/sterilization are summarized with a list of synthesis and design. Among them, the widely used silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are considered as the most effective bacterial agents in the type of nanomaterials at present and has been reported for inactivation of viruses, fungi, protozoa. Some methodologies against (1) disinfection by-products (DBPs) in traditional sterilization, (2) noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) agglomeration and release, (3) toxic metal leaching, (4) solar spectral response broadening, and (5) photogenerated e−/h+ pairs recombination are reviewed and discussed in this field, namely (1) alternative techniques and nanomaterials, (2) supporter anchoring effect, (3) nonmetal functional nanomaterials, (4) element doping, and (5) heterojunction constructing. The feasible strategies in the perspective of NDST are proposed to involve (1) non-noble metal disinfectors, (2) multi-functional nanomaterials, (3) multi-component nanocomposite innovation, and (4) hybrid techniques for disinfection/sterilization system. It is promising to achieve 100% bactericidal efficiency for 108 CFU/mL within a short time of less than 30 min.

ce:section.ce:para: Since 2019, the new coronavirus disease (Covid-19) has caused huge loss of human life and financial resources worldwide resulting to fact that the human being are caught in a panic of the pandemic. To this day, harmful microorganisms (i.e., viruses, fungi, protozoa, algae, and bacteria shown in Scheme 1 ) related infections have been the hotspot of concern. Throughout history, disasters caused by pathogen infections have been observed such as the plague causing by bacterium Yersinia pestis (Y. pestis), cholera by Vibrio cholerae (V. cholerae), tuberculosis by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (M. tuberculosis) (Staedel and Darfeuille, 2013). Statistically, enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (E. coli), a bacteria that causes diarrheal illness and is mainly transmitted through contaminated water, leads to over 1.3 million deaths of children under five all over the world annually (Santosham et al., 2010), and the number of deaths due to infection by harmful microorganisms has exceeded the deaths due to war, terrorism and weapons of mass destruction (Bartram et al., 2005).

In the past few decades, antibiotics have been proven to be the most effective way to treat pathogenic bacteria, which has led to the widespread use for anti-inflammatory. However, the abuse of antibiotics has caused many bacteria, such as Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Enterococcus faecium (E. faecium), to undergo genetic mutations so that antibiotics have begun to be ineffective due to antimicrobial resistance (Leeb, 2004). According to the published data in 2011, 25,000 patients died annually in the EU as a result of infections caused by antibiotic-resistant bacteria. There are two-thirds of these deaths because of Gram-negative pathogens (Szunerits and Boukherroub, 2016). In addition to the use of drugs, chlorination, ozonation, and ultraviolet disinfection have been widely used in the sterilization of water, air, food, and even other fields (Sharma et al., 2016; Vidic et al., 2019; Zhuang et al., 2015). However, the use of chlorination and ozonation reagents may produce potentially carcinogenic disinfection by-products (DBPs). Ultraviolet (UV) disinfection is considered a green method that does not produce toxic by-products, but it has a problem of high energy consumption and some of microorganisms are becoming inherently resistant to UV light. Considering the potential safety and environmental pollution issues, it is still an urgent desire to develop new antibacterial reagents and/or techniques with promising features in safety, efficiency, and environmental friendliness.

Nanomaterials are increasingly gaining attention as effective solution in antibacterial fields. They have been considered as one of the best candidates to defeat the presently challenging pathogens (Lin et al., 2018). Up to now, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been already used in daily life, medical, and military areas. AgNPs are considered as the most effective bacterial agents in the type of nanomaterials at present and has been reported for inactivation of viruses, fungi, protozoa, algae and bacteria (Rai et al., 2009). However, the consumption of AgNPs in size effects cannot be effectively avoided in aqueous system because the conversion of Ag0/Ag+ is occurring momentarily with Ag+ release and/or formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). As a kind of noble metal, that causes a great waste of material resources (Ding et al., 2021b; Le Ouay and Stellacci, 2015). On the other hand, AgNP's toxicity has been attracting attention in the past several years since it is easy to be dispersible nanoparticles and Ag+ ions (Fabrega et al., 2011).

Alternatively, photocatalytic disinfection technique employing TiO2 is considered to have great application prospects. This is a sustainably green technology driven by absorbing solar light (Matsunaga et al., 1985). As compared with bactericidal agents based on metal nanoparticles (e.g., AgNPs), semiconductors can work on stably and continuously in heterogeneous system because those nanomaterials are more tolerant towards extreme operating conditions and hard to dissolve (Etacheri et al., 2015). In order to promote the solar spectral response range of TiO2, many studies have adopted element doping to change its electronic structure. Although TiO2 doping can promote solar light utilization efficiency by narrowing bandgap and/or implanting valance/conduct bands, it will lead to high recombination of electron-hole (e−/h+) pairs. Otherwise, the doped elements are unstable and easy to leach since negative stress is existing in crystal structure invaded by external elements (Pelaez et al., 2012).

Hence the research direction is shifting towards the development of other nanomaterials, such as S-based (Wang and Mi, 2017), C-based (Perreault et al., 2015a; Wang et al., 2019b), N-based (Ong et al., 2016) nanomaterials. Among them, graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and graphene-doping as hotspots are extensively being investigated because they are nonmetal functional nanomaterials (Cha et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021). Nevertheless, AgNPs as a bactericidal agent in the aspect of bactericidal performance and practical application is unsurpassed so far. Therefore, a better strategy should be the development of nanocomposites integrating varied functional nanomaterials, wherein that is beneficial for inhibiting metal nanoparticles (e.g., AgNPs) consumption and enhancing efficiency of other components simultaneously (Hu et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2015). To the best of our knowledge, there is no overview on the comprehensive summarization of nanomaterial-based disinfection and sterilization other than those common metal (oxide) materials (i.e., AgNPs and TiO2) and graphene-based materials.

According to the keywords of nanomaterial, bactericidal behavior, disinfection technology, bactericidal devices, and sterilization product, total of ~200 publications for the type of review paper and research articles mainly in the years from 1998 to 2021 were reviewed. It aims to provide a timely review on the development of nanomaterial-based disinfection and sterilization in the past 20–30 years. The topics discussed herein include a brief summary of the types of nanomaterials with individual development progresses, and their bactericidal behaviors, multi-component nanocomposites and effect of physicochemical properties. Insights into nanomaterials antimicrobial activity on feasible technologies, products, devices, systems and scenarios are reviewed for the first time. The challenges and prospects of employing nanomaterials for control of harmful microorganisms and future development direction are also discussed.

In the history of mankind, high-temperature heating is the oldest disinfection method that has survived to this day, but with the development of society, it is no longer generally applicable, so the following three sterilization methods have emerged.

Chlorination disinfection refers to utilizing chemical reagents such as chlorine and chlorine dioxide for pathogens inactivation in water. Chlorine and chlorine dioxide, as high-effective oxidants, are used in medical, industrial, and military care for pathogen inactivation as well (Hamilton et al., 1996). However, chlorination has many drawbacks (He et al., 2019). Some studies have found that chlorination may induce a viable but unculturable (VBNC) state in bacteria, in which the bacteria lose their ability to culture but remain active and may return to metabolic activity (Lin et al., 2017). In addition, chlorination disinfection also produce other toxic DBPs which will increase the risk of cancer, and even harm the central nervous system (He et al., 2019).

Ozonation is used in wastewater treatment because of its excellent performance in removing odor, color, and various organic micro-pollutants, as well as the removal of various pathogens (Ding et al., 2019). Wu et al. studied the inactivation of E. coli in the ozone oxidation process and the formation of ozonation DBPs (e.g., bromate). The study found that the priority of inactivation efficiency determined by different cell viability methods was in the order of colony forming units (CFU) > membrane damage > DNA damage. Furthermore, the membrane/DNA damages for bacterial inactivation can be attributed to the direct O3 attacks (Ding et al., 2019). Typical DBPS produced by ozone oxidation are formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, glyoxal, methylglyoxal, and trichloroacetaldehyde (chloral hydrate), which are classified as by-products with some or all of the toxicological characteristics of human carcinogens, although they are not legally required in most countries (Park et al., 2016). Additionally, the high requirements for operation and maintenance and high costs also hinder the large-scale application of ozone oxidation.

Ultraviolet (UV) disinfection has attracted more and more attention because of its advantages, such as high disinfection efficiency, no toxic DBPs as outputs, and safe operation (Nourmoradi et al., 2012). UV radiation can change the DNA coding, thereby destroying the replication ability of microorganisms, and ultimately leading to their inactivation (Chen et al., 2019b). Nevertheless, UV disinfection still has some disadvantages (Chen et al., 2019b). The antibacterial effect depends on the exposure time and the intensity of UV radiation (Nourmoradi et al., 2012), which causes energy consumption with a high cost. Moreover, Chen et al. studied the conjugated transfer of antibiotic-resistance genes (ARGs) in antibiotic-resistance bacteria under different lighting conditions, and found that UV radiation induces stronger stimulation, leading to more significant oxidative stress, higher gene expression and stronger acceleration of ARG binding transfer (Chen et al., 2019b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced technology for sterilization and disinfection based on nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, four kinds nanomaterials research hotpots focusing on pathogen inactivation are summarized in Table 1 including M (metal)-based, S-based, C-based, and N-based materials and their innovative multi-component nanocomposites. As advanced technologies for pathogen disinfection, they have the advantages of avoiding DBPs, saving chemical agents and/or energy, enhancing inactivation ratios, minimizing size of devices and setups, and exploring application fields, simultaneously.

Silver nanomaterial has a wide range of applications, especially for wide investigation of its disinfection performance since the 20th century (Rai et al., 2009). During the past two decades, hundreds of articles have been reported on the bactericidal efficacy of Ag0 and Ag-containing species, films, coatings, composites and so on. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as a novel antibacterial agent can effectively work at a low concentration so that it is insignificant for the harmful impact caused. According to the reported results, antibacterial effect mainly depends on its size and morphology. The corresponding bactericidal mechanisms (Scheme 1) are proposed as (Fabrega et al., 2011; Le Ouay and Stellacci, 2015; Loo et al., 2013; Loo et al., 2015; Rai et al., 2009): (1) Release electrons in AgNPs can induce the degradation of lipopolysaccharide molecules, which leads to the changes in membrane permeability associated with nanoparticle penetration and intracellular fluid leakage; (2) AgNPs penetrate bacterial cells, causing interruption of intracellular metabolic pathways and even DNA damage; and (3) AgNPs transform into Ag+ followed by interacting with sulfhydryl groups in proteins, which leads to inactivation of respiratory enzymes due to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cell (Le Ouay and Stellacci, 2015). More recently, antibacterial research on AgNPs are extending to its composites because it not only can save costs and avoid risks (i.e., reduce Ag+ release) but also can involve multiple antibacterial effects, which will be reviewed in the following chapters.

In addition to silver, several metal or their oxides nanoparticles, such as Cu (CuO, Cu2O), Al (Al2O3), Bi (Bi2O3), Ce (CeO2), Fe (Fe2O3), Mg (MgO), Ti (TiO2), and Zn (ZnO) and mixed metal oxides (Hajipour et al., 2012; Kheiri et al., 2019; Nel et al., 2006; Slavin et al., 2017), have a potential as sterilization materials as well. The bactericidal behavior is nothing more than what mentioned above as the representative nanomaterial of AgNPs. Although the dosage of metal nanoparticles is rare in application at present, the concentration and absorption of heavy metal in eco-system is worthy of great attention. It might have a potential risk in human health through food chain (Qin et al., 2021). On the other hand, it does not work for all of metal nanoparticle such as gold (Au) (Byeon and Kim, 2014), meanwhile it does not work well for some inert metals without high-efficiency of electron release. Therefore, considering photo-bactericidal materials as the representative for the novel nanomaterials that has attracted a great deal of attention in life sciences and healthcare. For example, TiO2-based photocatalytical sterilization created by Fujishima is one of the most promising techniques for disinfection and sterilization (Sunada et al., 1998).

TiO2 as a kind of novel bactericidal material has attracted people's attention in 1985 (Matsunaga et al., 1985), while it has been reported that TiO2-based photocatalytic disinfection is also suitable for inhibiting waterborne viruses (Zhang et al., 2019a). Because it has some advantages such as low cost, promising stability, high disinfection efficiency, nontoxicity, and robust oxidizing capability (Etacheri et al., 2015). The corresponding bactericidal mechanisms (Scheme 1) are proposed as (Wei et al., 1994): (1) ROS generated on the surface of TiO2 derived by photogenerated e−/h+ pairs after absorbing solar light; (2) The ROS can be used to damage the cell wall and even interfere with DNA replication; and (3) In the photocatalytic process, hydrogen peroxide could also be generated in the TiO2 suspension, which can further increase ROS (e.g., HO) or directly interact with microorganisms. However, the wide band gap of TiO2 only can be excited by absorbing UV radiation (λ < 380 nm, it is only ~3% in solar energy on the surface of earth ground) (Cristian Vasquez et al., 2013). On the other hand, it will cause high energy consumption with high carbon emission rate when TiO2 drives photocatalysis by employing artificial UV lamps. In addition, the UV light itself has the effect of disinfecting and inactivating microorganisms which was mentioned in traditional sterilization technology (Section 2.3). To overcome these challenges, several strategies have been widely used to synthesize visible-light-driven TiO2-based photocatalyst such as doping (Pelaez et al., 2012), and inserting heterojunction structure (Kumar et al., 2020).

TiO2 indeed works on photocatalytic degradation of BPA and its antibacterial activity against E. coli. But it is found that TiO2 had poor responsiveness in visible light irradiation associated with shorter photogenerated e−/h+ recombination time (Kaushik et al., 2019b). Based on this, Al-doped TiO2 nanoflakes were synthesized in a single pot and tested their sterilizing effects in visible light (Kaushik et al., 2019a). The results showed that the doped TiO2 performed a higher microbe removal percentage than the pristine TiO2 under visible light irradiation (Table 1). Thus, metal-doping can indeed improve the photocatalytic capacity of TiO2. Furthermore, doping with metal ions such as bismuth makes the lifetime of photoexcited charge carriers much longer (Lin et al., 2013). Many studies have also shown that TiO2 doping with transition metals (Klosek and Raftery, 2001; Nikolay et al., 2011; Surowka et al., 2019), such as V, Fe, Cr, Nb, and Ni, can improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 by narrowing the bandgap of TiO2. In addition to metallic elements, some non-metal elements may also be doped. Nitrogen is frequently reported to narrow the bandgap of TiO2, with a consequent increase in the photoactivity under visible light (Abu Bakar and Ribeiro, 2016). Kawashita et al. discovered N-TiO2 samples synthesized under an NH3 atmosphere with bactericidal activity against E. coli after 60 min of visible-light irradiation (Kawashita et al., 2016). Doping B, C, and S also can improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 (Muhich et al., 2014). In addition, many efforts on multi-element co-doping for further enhancing photocatalysis of TiO2 were made. For example, co-doping of Zn and N can raise the photo-efficiency of TiO2, because the synergistic effect of co-doping can narrow the bandgap energy of TiO2 which makes it become visible-light-driven photocatalysts (Alotaibi et al., 2021).

TiO2 doping with element is an effective way to broaden its visible spectral response range. However, it will change the electronic structure of TiO2 with the band structure narrowing or a new band implanting, which leads to high recombination of e−/h+ pairs. After that, constructing heterojunction catalyst has attracted much attention (Kumar et al., 2020), which can further enhance TiO2 photocatalytic effect by reducing recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs. Metal (oxide) can enhance the e−/h+ separation and interface charge transfer capabilities of TiO2, and increase its excitation by visible light, so it is widely used in composites (Kubacka et al., 2008). For example, Liu et al. used a facile polyol method to deposit AgNPs on TiO2 nanofibers and successfully to fabricated AgNP/TiO2 nanofiber membrane, which achieved 99.9% bacteria (107 CFU/mL) inactivation under solar irradiation within 30 min (Liu et al., 2012). The hybrid Cu2O/TiO2 photocatalytic nanocomposites with p-n heterojunction reported by Li et al. can achieve ~100% sterilization within 30 min (Li et al., 2019a). According to the photogenerated holes acting as the main active species, Cu2O/TiO2 driven bactericidal behavior could induce the leakage of K+ ions and seriously damage the cell structure, which leads to the cell death completely. In other reports, TiO2 is decorated with Au (Byeon and Kim, 2014), Bi (-based) (Liu et al., 2017), Fe (-based) (Ma et al., 2015), and other metal (oxide) to improve the photocatalytic sterilization of pristine TiO2. Moreover, there have been many studies on the photocatalytic effects of TiO2-nonmetal heterojunction materials. In the experiment of C-TiO2 nanocomposites photocatalyst (He et al., 2021), TiO2 nanoparticles were anchored on the carbon layer, the results showed that carbon can not only prevent the agglomeration of TiO2, but also can make the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs efficiently separated. Therefore, the lifespan of e−/h+ was prolonged in the transfer process so as to improve the photocatalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of bactericidal nanomaterials in metal (oxides)
ce:section.ce:para: Besides TiO2, ZnO is another strong photocatalyst because it can absorb a greater part of the UV spectrum (Reddy et al., 2007; Shekofteh-Gohari et al., 2018). The studies on ZnO/metal nanocomposites in photocatalytic sterilization have been more in-depth research in the past decades. In contrast with pure ZnO (He et al., 2014), the deposition of Au on the surface of ZnO resulted in an increase in sterilization rate of E. coli and S. aureus by simulated sunlight radiation. In the ZnO/Au hybrid nanostructures, Au played the key function of (1) enhancing light absorption because of the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Au, (2) changing the valence and conduction band structure of ZnO and thereby promoting reactivity of photoinduced charge carriers, and (3) increasing the efficiency of electron transport and charge carrier separation. Among them, researchers revealed that the most important reason is the changes in valence and conduction band structure with Au implantation, which is the basic reason for the enhancement of electron transport and spectral response/absorption.

In addition, many metal (oxides), such as Ag (Bechambi et al., 2015), Cu (Kumar et al., 2014), Co (Poongodi et al., 2015), Ni (Uma et al., 2019), Al (-based) (Borker and Gaokar, 2020), and Mn (-based) (Yu et al., 2016), were applied for ZnO modification to improve its photocatalytic sterilization activity. Moreover, the surface coating of metal (oxide) nanoparticles using biopolymer chitosan has evolved to become an important area of polymer nanotechnology, which is mainly attributed to the unique characteristics of chitosan such as biodegradability, biocompatibility, and antimicrobial properties (Kassem et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2017). In a recent study by Bharathi et al. (Bharathi et al., 2019), a biopolymer chitosan-coated ZnO nanocomposite was prepared for the first time by using rutin. The prepared CS-ZnO nanocomposite showed antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial pathogens.

More recently the trend of ferrite nanomaterials as a new kind of bactericidal catalyst has been attracting considerable attention in water disinfection. Compared with traditional iron-based materials, spinel ferrites (Kefeni and Mamba, 2020) (MFe2O4; M = Zn, Cu, Co, Mg, etc.) own better physicochemical stability, enable easier catalyst recovery and reuse due to their magnetic properties. Moreover, it can be useful as catalysts for production of ROS (e.g., HO) under visible light irradiation by photocatalysis or in dark by Fenton-like oxidation (Qu et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 1 , a process diagram of water disinfection employing ferrite nanomaterials can involve (1) water disinfection in the presence of ferrites, solar light and H2O2, (2) magnetic separation and recovery of ferrites, and (3) discharge of treated water. However, it has been found that ferrite nanomaterials performed a weak sterilization efficiency (Hu et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2012). Because (1) photoinduced e−/h+ pairs of bare ferrites tend to recombine rapidly due to their narrow bandgaps; and (2) ferrite nanomaterials with ferromagnetism are easy to agglomerate.

To overcome the shortcomings, a coupling strategy is one of the feasible ways. When ZnFe2O4 was combined with AgI, its photocatalytic sterilization efficiency can reach at ~100% within 80 min (Xu et al., 2018). On the other hand, the agglomeration of nanoparticles with their morphology and particle size distribution (PSD) can be controlled and stable by the addition of polymer chelating/gelling agent (Zahraei et al., 2016). A study on cobalt ferrite/chitosan nanohybrid materials embodied a good antibacterial via a synergy of the functions of cobalt ferrites and chitosan, in which the presence of chitosan boosts the interaction time between cobalt ferrite and bacteria (El-Shahawy et al., 2018). Moreover, a novel antibacterial platform namely as photothermal-catalytic antibacterial therapy (PCAT) driven by fabricating hemoglobin-functionalized copper ferrite nanoparticles (Hb-CFNPs) was proposed in the first time (Liu et al., 2019). This experiment combines the Fenton-like system and the high temperature effect of near infrared (NIR at 808 nm) to exacerbate successfully the death of bacteria (E. coli of 100%, S. aureus of 96.4%).

MoS2 as a classic S-based nanomaterial for sterilization and disinfection has been becoming a hot topic in recent years (Wang and Mi, 2017; Yin et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. S1, the MoS2 monolayer consists of Mo atom plane sandwiched between two planes of S atoms (Radisavljevic et al., 2011). The interlayer spacing between Mo plane is around 0.62 nm while the free spacing is 0.30 nm. The bulk MoS2 is composed of layered structure stacked by strong covalent MoS bonds but the van der Waals forces between layers are weak. Up to recent years, two-dimensional (2D) MoS2 with nanomaterial properties which distinguished from bulk MoS2 were discovered (Krishnan et al., 2019). It was found that the band edge position for layered MoS2 can be changed by adjusting the number of layers of MoS2 nanosheets. Thus the redox electricity produced for the formation of ROS can be excited through MoS2 absorbing solar spectrum in a different wavelength range (Shivaji et al., 2018). In bactericidal behavior of MoS2, ROS effect on microorganism inactivation is similar to those mentioned in above sections wherein the ROS mainly generated in photocatalysis or peroxidase catalytic activity (Yin et al., 2016). The other sterilization mechanisms of MoS2 can be cell membrane damage by sharp MoS2 edges and photothermal inactivation (Roy et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021).

MoS2-based nanomaterial antibacterial experiments under light radiation have been extensively studied. An excellent discovery that the antibacterial activity of MoS2 materials depended on size effect is ranked as follows: MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) > MoS2 nanosheets > bulk MoS2, which is consistent with the level of generated ROS (Tian et al., 2019). Roy et al. found that MoS2 sheets act as the photothermal sensor which can induce bacterial cell death or inactivation by encapsulating cells under the impact of near-infrared radiation, they also proved that the physical contact between MoS2 sheets and bacteria further aggravated this result (Roy et al., 2019). In another study (Yang et al., 2020), Yang et al. reported that the introduction of C atom into ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets can enhance bactericidal efficiency of MoS2 by preventing its agglomeration, improving light absorption, changing the energy structure, or being conducive to the generation, separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons. On the other hand, MoS2 also performed bactericidal effect in the absence of light. In Tang et al. (Tang et al., 2020), vertically aligned MoS2 nanosheets were coated on titanium substrates via a one-step hydrothermal reaction, and their excellent activity against both E. coli and S. aureus was observed under dark conditions. They evidenced that the antimicrobial efficacy of such MoS2 nanosheets is ascribed to both ROS generation and MoO4 2− release.

In addition, CdS and WS2 have distinctive physicochemical properties, namely native vacancy defects, which makes them have a promising application prospects in the field of photocatalysis such as photocatalytic H2 evolution, photocatalytic sterilization (Lin et al., 2019; Shang et al., 2017). CdS is a typical metal‑sulfur group semiconductor with the bandgap of ~2.4 eV. The release of ROS and toxic ions leads to the antibacterial activity of CdS (Shang et al., 2017). Recently, the biological synthesis for CdS preparation has attracted more attention because of its simplicity, eco-friendliness, and extended antimicrobial activity of CdS. Ayodhya et al. first tried to synthesize CdS nanoparticles by using Calotropis gigantea leaf extract (Ayodhya and Veerabhadram, 2017). The addition of the extract enhanced the antibacterial activity of CdS, which was mainly attributed to the increase of ROS. Integrated with other reports on the mechanism of antibacterial activity of CdS NPs, the effect of intracellular ROS generation on membrane lipid peroxidation in microorganism is highly significant (Nasrin et al., 2019). WS2 is another important S-based materials as a kind of p-type semiconductor with a bandgap of ~2.0 eV. Unfortunately, the narrow bandgap results in a weak photocatalytic activity of WS2 materials due to the fast recombination of carriers (Kim et al., 2017). To overcome this drawback, a strategy on co-catalyst for accelerating the transfer of carriers to slow the recombination was suggested. For example, WS2 nanosheets decorated with Ag2S nanoparticles (Ag2S/WS2) showed high-effective antibacterial efficacy within 20 min of over 99% towards S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (Lin et al., 2019).

In addition to the above three S-based nanomaterials for sterilization and disinfection, indium-based ternary metal sulfide (In-TMS) could be seen as a novel nanomaterials for photocatalytic sterilization (Hu et al., 2022). It has been reported that the antibacterial capacity was in the order of ZnIn2S4 > CdIn2S4 > AgInS2. The ROS formed was elaborated all of HO, O2
–, and 1O2 in ZnIn2S4 but only part in CdIn2S4 or AgInS2 (He et al., 2015).

Carbon-based nanomaterials (e.g., carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, carbon black, etc.) have attracted extensive attention due to their features of the metal-free and outstanding physicochemical properties. They can be widely used in the field of biosensors, solar thermal fuel, electrochemical energy storage, and sterilization/disinfection (He et al., 2012; Hu et al., 2021; Mauter and Elimelech, 2008; Novoselov et al., 2012; Novoselov et al., 2004). Among C-based nanomaterials, fullerenes (C60) and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as a pioneering carbon nanomaterials for antibacterial study have been unfold a new direction (Mauter and Elimelech, 2008). Subsequently, a new wave of research upsurge on graphene was quickly detonated since 2004 (Novoselov et al., 2004), it is a 2D ultra-thin nanocarbon material reported as an emerging antibacterial material (Novoselov et al., 2012). In the family members of graphene, the functional graphene nanomaterials (FGNs) include single and multilayer graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduction graphene oxide (RGO), graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQD) and corresponding organic/inorganic compound hybrid nanocarbon composites (Liu et al., 2011; Zheng et al., 2015). Recently, some studies revealed that GO has potential application for inhibiting coronavirus infectivity by interacting with virus surface proteins and cell receptors (Unal et al., 2021).

A three-staged cell membrane damage based on contact interaction between graphene and E. coli has been validated by using transmission electron microscope (TEM) technique. These three stages basically showed bacteria undergoing resistance stage, bacterial fragmentation, and then death (Tu et al., 2013). After that, some graphene derivatives (e.g., GO and RGO nanosheets) can effectively inhibit the growth of E. coli as well, while showing minimal cytotoxicity (Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2010; Hu et al., 2010). The bactericidal mechanism (Fig. 2 ) based on nanocarbon materials (e.g., graphene-based) through contact interaction could work as (Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2010; Pham et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2019): (1) incision into the cell membrane by the sharp edges caused physical damage, (2) lipid extraction due to biocompatibility of carbon-based materials, (3) wrapping the cell, and (4) introducing oxidative stress towards destroy protein, DNA and tissue via cell membrane penetration. It is generally believed that the oxidative stress is mediated by reactive oxygen species (ROS). However, the pristine C-based nanomaterials are hard to form ROS because their electronic structure is stable (Lyon et al., 2008). To enhance the sterilization and disinfection of C-based nanomaterials, facile strategies including heteroatoms doping and composites are adopted (Putri et al., 2017; Sahoo et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2014). Taking graphene as an example, the following is an introduction on how to strengthen it by the mentioned strategies above.

Graphene doped with heteroatoms (N, B, S, etc.) become an emerging strategy to enhance electronic properties of FGNs (Putri et al., 2017). In contrast to pristine graphene, the doped graphene provides active sites for electron donation, which improves the formation of ROS.

In terms of N doping, the similarity is in the atomic size between N and C that makes a facile replacement of C with N in the graphene lattice by forming strong covalent bonds. Meanwhile, N with five available valance electrons in the outermost electron orbital which makes graphene lattice become an n-type structure and easier electron donation relatively (Wang et al., 2014). A system of modified N-doped graphene (NG) materials was synthesized by hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2019c). The biological activity of NG samples was evaluated by oxidizing intracellular antioxidant glutathione (GSH) and bacterial viability (E. coli). Oxidative stress is considered to be the main antibacterial mechanism. Similarly, S or O with more valance electrons was investigated on graphene doping. S-doped GQDs have a wider adsorption range and higher photo-generated charge separation rate in the visible light region compared with S-free GQDs, so the photocatalytic performance is stronger (Huang et al., 2018). Besides, the mechanistic insights towards the antimicrobial activity of O-doped graphene has been demonstrated by study on both fungi and bacteria sterilization and sensitivity (Maktedar et al., 2016). Moreover, Kim et al. studied N and B co-doped RGO, they found that co-doping can further increase the number density of catalytic active sites, which greatly improves the catalytic activity of graphene (Kim et al., 2019).

Graphene anchored functional nanomaterials is a typical method to enhance bactericidal performance, while it can also prevent agglomeration of the mentioned nanomaterials. For example, AgNPs agglomeration can be inhibited by graphene as show in Fig. 2 (at top right corner). Sahoo et al. reported that a freeze-casting method was successfully used to synthesize 3D-graphene/Ag nanocomposites (Sahoo et al., 2018). This graphene-based silver nanocomposite showed a sterilization rate of 100% after 3 h targeted at a certain microorganism. Reduced GO (i.e., RGO) has better conductivity and electron transfer performance than that of GO, which is probably ascribed to electron transfer and Ag-releasing suppression. It is found that RGO anchored AgNPs (10–100 nm) showed a good bactericidal performance with a long lifespan (Chen et al., 2019a).

Graphene in combination with metal oxides (or sulfides) shows some of advantages in terms of light energy absorption, anti-recombination of e−/h+ pairs, surface modification, anti-agglomeration, and catalyst stability. That is attributed to the reasons for enhancing bactericidal behavior. TiO2 decorated RGO made the surface charges of TiO2 become positive, which improves the interaction between TiO2 and bacteria (Raja et al., 2019). In ZnO/RGO nanocomposites, the composites performed a higher absorption in UV radiation with a better sterilization (Kavitha et al., 2012). Moreover, the Fe3O4/RGO nanocomposites showed better antimicrobial activity against three bacterial pathogens such as S. aureus, B. subtilis, and E. coli compared to GO (Padhi et al., 2017). On the other hand, RGO anchored FeS clusters can suppress Fe2+ release for prolonging lifespan of FeS nanomaterials (Ali et al., 2020). Up to this day, some semiconductors nanoparticles as an antimicrobial agent, like CdS (Deng et al., 2016), Ag3PO4 (Yang et al., 2015), and Bi2MoO6 (Zhang et al., 2013) among others have been explored in combination with graphene.

Graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is an attractive metal-free N-type semiconductor with a variety of unique electrical, optical, structural, and physicochemical properties, which makes it has a broad application prospect in the fields of electronics, catalysis, environmental remediation, and biocidal (Mei et al., 2020; Ong et al., 2016). There are many reports on the sterilization and virus elimination based on g-C3N4 nanomaterials' photocatalysis. In the processes, ROS formed can lead to cell death and the reduction of microbial load with the leakage of minerals, genetic material, and proteins (Murugesan et al., 2019). O2
– radical species were demonstrated as the initial ROS formed in g-C3N4 photocatalysis to inactivate the virus under visible light irradiation. Heo et al. evaluated the antibacterial activity of 2D g-C3N4 photocatalyst against waterborne E. coli, S. aureus, and S. typhimurium, and found that antibacterial activity and ROS production depend on the type of N-functional groups and the specific surface area (Heo et al., 2019).

However, pure g-C3N4 can no longer target at the bactericidal demands. Many attempts have been made to overcome the limitations of g-C3N4 by doping (non-)metals elements or by coupling with other functional components (Guo et al., 2021). As mentioned in above sections, metal (oxides), as widely used Ag-based disinfector, is one of the most important participants in the design of antibacterial nanocomposites. Compared with the bulk g-C3N4, the AgO/g-C3N4 with narrow bandgap can be enhanced at absorbing visible light effectively and bactericidal rate with the increase of the content of AgO (Shen et al., 2019). Furthermore, Ag/PCNO (porous g-C3N4), has a sterilization rate of 99.4% against S. aureus (Xu et al., 2019). The Ag@g-C3N4 NSs reported can be used as an alternative to traditional antibiotics to treat severe diseases related to P. aeruginosa (Khan et al., 2018). In addition to silver, some researches focus on other g-C3N4-based composites, such as g-C3N4/TiO2 (Li et al., 2015) and g-C3N4/MnO2 (Wu et al., 2020), to remove microorganisms.

Boron nitride (BN), exhibiting graphene-based structural analogies, is another N-based nanomaterial for potential sterilization and disinfection (Li et al., 2010). BN nanoflakes integrated with low-density polyethylene (LDPE) can lead to irreparable physical damage on microorganisms by extracting phospholipids, causing severe membrane stress and damaging cell integrity (Zhan et al., 2019).

To avoid the limitation of solo nanomaterial on disinfection and sterilization, multi-component nanocomposites (with or without heterojunction structure) fabrication is an ultra-effective innovative way to make up for the disadvantages (i.e., agglomeration of metal (oxide) nanoparticles, recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, etc.) and improve the antibacterial efficiency of materials. Among them, graphene and g-C3N4 as supports are most extensively investigated in the past decade (Table 2 ). The performance on disinfection and sterilization for harmful microorganisms can reach a higher efficiency via synergistic effect of the above mentioned bactericidal mechanism. For examples, a fabricated NN heterojunction structure based on the ZnFe2O4/g-C3N4 combined with polypyrrole (PPY) was demonstrated to be better carrier transfer in the photocatalytic process (Das et al., 2020). Moreover, Chen et al. reported a facile hydrothermal method for the preparation of a Z-scheme silver‑silver bromide/g-C3N4 @ nitrogen-doped graphene aerogel (Ag-AgBr/g-C3N4@NGA) 3D porous composite, which has a disinfection effect on E. coli (approximately 6 log inactivation) and S. aureus (~1.2 log inactivation) in 60 min under visible-light illumination (Chen et al., 2019c).

Recently, a novel 0D-2D-3D nanostructured RGO wrapping AgNP/Bi2Fe4O9 (BFOA-G) composite was successfully designed and fabricated in a previous work (Hu et al., 2018). The sterilization efficiency on E. coli can reach 100% within 20 min. As shown in Fig. 3a, this ternary nanocomposites are composed of visible-light sensitive catalyst BFO (Bi2Fe4O9), AgNPs decoration on the surface, and RGO sheet wrapping. There are four co-existing sterilization mechanisms based on BFOA-G in the presence of visible light irradiation and H2O2 systems including RGO assisted Ag+ release, Ag assisted Fenton reaction, Ag/RGO assisted photocatalysis, and Ag assisted photo-Fenton oxidation (Fig. 3b).

Furthermore, a 3D RGO/WS2 nanosheet/Mg-doped ZnO nanohybrid (RGOWMZ) was synthesized using electrostatic assembly method by a layer-by-layer elaborate design on microscale (Chen et al., 2017). In this kind of nanocomposites, the purpose of Mg doping is to generate an intermediate energy gap between the valence and conduct bands of ZnO, so as to promote the absorption of photons and to create more e−/h+ pairs. WS2 nanosheet introduced can work with Mg-ZnO for achieving heterojunction structure, which is beneficial for anti-recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs. 3D RGO as the support mainly functions as carriers transmitter and a place of bactericidal interaction.

As shown in Table 2, there are more representative nanocomposites for sterilization and disinfection in the past decade. It implies that the development of efficient sterilizing materials has become an important trend.

The antimicrobial characteristics of nanomaterials were closely related to their physicochemical properties. Chemical oxidative stress is one of the important mechanisms in sterilization, which can cause the oxidation of intracellular components including proteins, phospholipids, etc. The oxidizing ability mainly depends on the physicochemical properties of nanomaterials, i.e., size, shape, surface ion valence state, or structure (Moritz and Geszke-Moritz, 2013).

In graphene-based materials, it is reported that the effect of GO size on antibacterial properties is very significant (Perreault et al., 2015b). The antibacterial properties increase with the decrease in the size of GO sheets, which is mediated by its enhancement of oxidative stress due to a high defect density introduced in GO sheets. On the contrary, in the cell suspension, the sterilization of bacteria is upto GO wrapping way. The bactericidal efficiency of GO increased with increasing sheet size. Results reflected that the size of nanomaterials has different effects on the sterilization performance under different conditions. In addition, the toxicity of silver nanomaterials is also size-dependent (Fabrega et al., 2011). Small-size AgNP (D90 < 20 nm) showed a better bactericidal performance than its large-size (D90 > 100 nm). Besides the mechanism mentioned, the surface-to-volume ratio is proportional to the size of nanomaterials and they can easily penetrate into cells with a smaller size (Hsiao et al., 2015).

The shape of nanomaterials is another important parameter in antibacterial activities. It is already reported that an important reason in the difference of antibacterial activity for varied carbon materials (e.g., single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), GO, RGO, and C60) is their unique shape (Mauter and Elimelech, 2008). It is demonstrated that needle-shaped SWCNT and knife-shaped GO are beneficial to enhance performance in sterilization compared with the spherical C60. For S-based nanomaterials, Shalabayev et al. synthesized the spherical and needle-like copper sulfide (CuS) nanocrystals via a sulfur-mediated mechanochemical method (Shalabayev et al., 2019). It was found that needle-like CuS can obtain sterilization against both E. coli and S. aureus bacteria, whereas the spherical CuS was only good against E. coli. Moreover, Hong et.al uncovered that the shape effect on the antibacterial activity of Ag nanomaterials can be attributed to the effective contact area and surface reactivity, the antibacterial capacity was in the order of Ag nanoparticles > Ag nanocubes > Ag nanowires (Hong et al., 2016).

More recently, there has been considerable focus on the effect of valance state of metal ions on bactericidal efficiency in nanomaterials. It has been reported that a kind of nanozymes was designed by copper-based nanomaterials and carbon materials (Xi et al., 2020). The different valance state of cupper ions (i.e., Cu and CuO) were successfully decorated on carbon materials, respectively. It is found that Cu/carbon nanozymes exhibited a higher antibacterial activity than that of CuO/carbon, because they resulted in different bactericidal mechanism. To achieve membrane damage, lipid peroxidation, or DNA degradation, the former effectively generate ROS through peroxidase-like catalytic reaction between Cu0/Cu1+, Cu0/Cu2+ and Cu1+/Cu2+ simultaneously, whereas the second is only through Cu2+/Cu1+ with a slow conversion rate.

The different crystal structures of a same nanomaterial show a varied sterilization effect. As far as the former is concerned, TiO2 is a good example, it has three types of crystal structure namely rutile, anatase, and brookite. The first two have the diversity in photocatalytic activity with different bandgap since it is distinguished from their electronic structure. In the field of catalytic sterilization, the researchers also found that the anatase shows a better antibacterial efficiency by photocatalysis than that of rutile (Caratto et al., 2013). In addition, the structure of MoS2 will also have a great impact on the sterilization effect. Compared with the 2H phase of MoS2, the 1 T phase shows better catalytic performance, which is due to the outstanding electron transport ability in 1 T-MoS2. It is confirmed that the electron concentration and conductivity of 1 T-MoS2 are 101–7 times higher than that of 2H-MoS2, which greatly reduces the e−/h+ recombination rate (Shivaji et al., 2018).

In addition, there are many efforts to study other influences of nanomaterials on sterilization and disinfection, such as surface functional groups (Liu et al., 2011), surface roughness (Zou et al., 2017), hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity (Ellinas et al., 2017) and so on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application products, devices, systems and scenarios

ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial devices can be rationally designed based on the physicochemical and biocidal properties for the above introduced nanomaterials. Some innovative devices have shown great promise for practical applications. In the following section, by focusing on electrode sterilization, disinfection filter and hybrid technology for sterilization and disinfection system, the progress in nanomaterial-based antimicrobial devices for environmental disinfection and microbial control is reviewed in detail.

Most of the nanomaterials mentioned have excellent electrical conductivity, so they have a potential to be assembled into electrodes for sterilization. In particular, C-based nanomaterial is more frequently investigated for electrode sterilization.

Recently, Singh et al. embedded sulfur-doped graphene into a polymer dense membrane or porous substrate (Singh et al., 2017). It was used to make an electrode for the removal of P. aeruginosa in water. The result showed that the sterilization efficiency of the electrode is directly related to the voltage, and the sterilization effect can reach 100% at 2.5 V. Moreover, Wang et al. invented an economical and green sterilization device that is a high-performance capacitive deionization disinfection (CDID) electrode by employing an activated carbon (AC) electrode coated with cationic nanohybrids of graphene oxide-graft-quaternized chitosan (GO-QC) (Wang et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 4 , the water disinfection process is continuous requiring only a small electrode voltage of 2 V. Furthermore, a 3D activated graphene electrode was modified by carboxymethyl cellulose as negative charge and disinfectant quaternary ammonium cellulose with positively charged polyatomic ions of the structure (El-Deen et al., 2016). Under a certain condition, the results indicated that the electrode couple graphene nanomaterial exhibited abnormal electrosorption capacity and had good antibacterial properties.

Besides, metal (oxide) was successfully fabricated for electron sterilization. For example, Tian et al. set up a self-powered and high-efficient water sterilization system (Fig. 5 ), which consists of a wave-driven triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) and two nanobrush electrodes made of AgNPs integrated ZnO-nanowires (Tian et al., 2017). A variety of microorganisms (including microorganisms in natural river water) have shown immediate and sustainable sterilization performance. The electricity generated by TENG was applied to Ag/ZnO electrodes, and the bacterial solution was passed through electrodes at a constant speed of 1 mL/min driven by a peristaltic pump and was collected dropwise. Within 0.5 min of treating Gram-negative bacteria in the electric field, CFU decreased from 106 to 0. In addition, the ability to kill bacteria was sustained for at least 20 min after removing the electric field.
More recently, S-based and N-based nanomaterials were also implanted into electrodes in research works on sterilization and disinfection (Praveena et al., 2019; Wang and Mi, 2017).

Compared with electrodes sterilization, disinfection filters are more widely used. In 2004, Srivastava et al. used a continuous spray pyrolysis method to produce a monolithic uniform macro hollow cylinder with radially aligned CNT walls and use it as a filter, which exhibits excellent resistance to E. coli (Srivastava et al., 2004). The CNT filter can be cleaned and regenerated by simple ultrasonic and autoclave sterilization. For performance and cost considerations, nanocomposites containing 97% polyvinyl-N-carbazole (PVK) and only 3% single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were used for preparing filter (Ahmed et al., 2013). The sterilization results show that the removal rate of Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria is 80–90%, and the virus removal efficiency is 2.5 logs. In another case (Fig. S2), antibacterial chitosan/AgNPs (CS/AgNPs) cork particles were packed in the column (shown in the right side) (Garcia Pena et al., 2017). The results show that bacteria can be completely removed within 8 h in the circulating filtration mode. In addition, the results revealed that the structure of E. coli is affected by the surface-modified cork filter media with antibacterial nanomaterials, which prevents the attachment and proliferation of E. coli and enhances the purification effect. Moreover, a thin-film nanocomposite membrane (TFNCM), which contained GOQD/AP nanocomposite, showed a sterilization rate of 99.9%. Wherein GOQD was decorated with silver phosphate (AP) by a facile electrostatically driven method followed by embedded into the dense polyamide layer via interfacial polymerization reaction (Li et al., 2019b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid technology for sterilization and disinfection system
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, some studies on hybrid technologies for sterilization and disinfection system were reported for anti-microorganism in the past decade (McEvoy and Zhang, 2014). They perform excellent sterilization towards viruses, fungi, protozoa, algae, and bacteria.

TiO2 surface-modified colloidal dispersion was directly coated on UV-lamp for integrating UV radiation with TiO2 photocatalysis for sterilization and disinfection. Meanwhile, O2 bubbling into the TiO2/UV radiation system could further enhance the sterilization performance of G. lamblia. It was attributed to the hybrid technology for bactericidal effect by cross-coupling UV radiation and ROS attack (generation based on TiO2 photocatalysis). Wherein, Guo et al. placed a thin membrane (composed of TiO2 tubular ceramic microfilter in Fig. S3) and an UV lamp at the focus of the two alumina parabolic reflectors. The parabolic design ensured that the outer surface of the membrane is radiated with uniform UV rays (Guo et al., 2015). The viruses can be inactivated and removed through UV radiation, TiO2 photocatalysis, and filtering effect, simultaneously.

In another study, T. Schoen et al. developed a novel technology for sterilization and disinfection system by combining electric field and nanomaterials (e.g., Ag and CNTs) (Schoen et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. 6 , a wide mouth funnel is energized with ultra-effective and high-throughput inactivation of bacteria in water. In this device, silver nanowires (AgNWs) and CNTs were implanted into cotton filter to improve bactericidal effect associated with the nanomaterials' antibiosis and conductivity. When the device is operated at 100,000 L/hm2, 98% of bacteria can be inactivated within only several seconds of total incubation time.

According to the mentioned antibacterial functional nanomaterials above, the developed products, devices and systems have a great potential application in the field of water security (Wang et al., 2019b), air purification (Jung et al., 2011), medical healthcare (Ding et al., 2021a), daily necessities (Kharaghani et al., 2018), textiles (Shahid ul et al., 2016), and so on. Moreover, it is no doubt that the nanomaterial-based photocatalytic disinfection technique also has a great promising application for protecting human health from waterborne, airborne, and foodborne viruses (Habibi-Yangjeh et al., 2020). Selected aspects closely related to the daily life of human beings are briefly reviewed.

World Health Organization (WHO) reported that there are more than half a billion people who lack access to safe drinking water and millions of mortalities occur due to waterborne pathogens (WHO, 2015). In world, water security has always been the focus of human survival including domestic water use (e.g., drinking water safety), outdoor emergency water use, water reservoir, and water resources. The novel devices based on nanomaterials has been mentioned in Section 4.1, while the innovated products in water disinfection are composite membrane (Liu et al., 2020), coatings (Noureen et al., 2020) and so on.

Today, membrane technology has been widely used not only in wastewater treatment but also in domestic drinking water. The greatest challenge is to avoid the generation of biofouling. Incorporation of bactericidal nanomaterials in development of antibacterial membrane for biofouling mitigation is one of the efficient strategies. The composite membranes have been investigated by introducing metals nanoparticles (Ma et al., 2016), CNTs (Tiraferri et al., 2011), GO (Liu et al., 2020) and others. Musico et al. have successfully decorated membrane using graphene-based nanomaterials on the surface of membrane filters. It showed an effective antibacterial performance with the formation of ROS (Musico et al., 2014). In addition, a highly efficient spray- and spin- assisted layer-by-layer (SSLbL) method was used to prepare a special reverse osmosis (RO) membranes with controllable loading of CuNPs for biofouling control (Ma et al., 2016). It is demonstrated that CuNPs could significantly improve the anti-flocculation performance of membranes and effectively inhibit the permeate flux reduction caused by bacterial deposition on the membrane surface. Similarly, a composite membrane containing AgNPs is significant to alleviate the generation of biofouling in the process of water treatment (Beisl et al., 2019).

On the other hand, nanomaterial in the form of coatings is another way for water security. Multifunctional textiles were obtained through coating Ag3PO4-RGO nanocomposites (Noureen et al., 2020). The textile can float on the water surface and decompose organic dyes and disinfect the pathogenic microorganisms in the water (the inhibition rate of E. coli was more than 99%) by absorbing solar energy. Studies also reported a novel coating that can be used for anti-biofouling on the bottom of ships (Iris and Lam, 2019; Pistone et al., 2016). This nano-coating based on unsaturated polyester resin and MWCNTs shows excellent antibacterial properties and mechanical durability. Besides, Oder et al. used Cu-doped TiO2 nanotube coatings to sterilize water piping systems (Oder et al., 2020). This experiment showed that the Cu/TiO2 nanotube coatings can prevent the growth of bacteria in static and flowing media when irradiated with UV.

Indoor air pollution has received great attention since the early 1990s. Studies have shown that indoor pollutant levels are higher than outdoors (Jones, 1999). Almost all people spend nearly 80% of their time indoors and are more likely to inhale pollutants. According to the WHO, air pollution causes about 3.3 million deaths worldwide each year, of which a large proportion of people die from airborne microorganisms (e.g., bacteria, viruses). Air purification technologies in sterilizing harmful microorganisms are imminently desirable at residential areas and large-scale public places (e.g., office, hospital, sports venue, education place, etc.). The popular products are air filters, air cleaners, and air disinfectant, wherein nanomaterials can play a significant role in bactericidal effect.

Jung et al. prepared Ag-coated CNT hybrid nanoparticles (Ag/CNTs) using aerosol nebulization and thermal evaporation/condensation processes and deposited them continuously onto an air filter medium (Jung et al., 2011). They found that the deposition of Ag/CNTs on the surface of the air filter media significantly increased the antibacterial activity of the test bacterial bioaerosol compared with the deposition of CNTs or AgNPs solely, whereas the filter pressure drop and bioaerosol filtration efficiency was similar to those of CNTs deposition. Moreover, Heo et al. developed an eco-friendly bioaerosol filtration system for indoor environments. Natural-product nanoparticles produced by Euscaphis japonica extract, were deposited onto air filters for sterilizing airborne bacteria (Hwang et al., 2015). More recently, Wang et al. prepared polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and konjac glucomannan (KGM)-based nanofiber membranes that loaded with ZnO nanoparticles by green electrospinning and ecofriendly thermal cross-linking (Lv et al., 2019). The obtained fiber membrane has high efficiency of air filtration and photocatalytic activity for antibacterial activity.

In air purification process, the influence of environmental factors such as temperature and humidity cannot be neglected. Molki et al. have studied a type of cross-linked protein nano fabrics for multifunctional air filtration at varying environmental conditions (Souzandeh et al., 2017). The results showed that compared with the one with no cross-linking, the cross-linking for the protein nanofibers can dramatically improve the environmental stability with moisture and temperature, without losing much the filtering performance of particulate pollutants and chemical pollutants.

With the development of technology, medical care has attracted more and more attention. In healthcare, there are some emergency scenarios such as surgical wound, incision would, empyrosis, and others. To avoid infections, to promote cell proliferation, and to accelerate wound healing, externally applied medical products for bacteria growth inhibition are sought.

Hydrogel-based product in medical applications has attracted a great deal of attention. Mao et al. applied a simple two-step method to embed Ag/Ag@AgCl/ZnO hybrid nanostructures into a hydrogel (Mao et al., 2017). The prepared hydrogel can accelerate wound healing and achieve rapid sterilization. Dai et al. designed a smart pH-responsive nanocomposite hydrogel via a Schiff base linkage between oxidized polysaccharides and cationic dendrimers encapsulated with silver nanoparticles to combat local bacterial infections associated with surgical wound and implanted medical devices (Dai et al., 2018). The nanocomposite hydrogel showed potent antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Analogously, Mai et al. developed a hydrogel-based nano-delivery system with antibacterial activity and skin regeneration function, which was used for burn infections (Mai et al., 2021). The experimental results confirmed that multifunctional hydrogel successfully inhibited infection and concurrently promoted wound healing.

In addition to the hydrogel-based products, the coating is another widely used product in life. Fan et al. found that the LbL-assembled Ag-graphene‑sodium alginate sulfate nanocomposite coating had good bactericidal activity, blood compatibility, and the ability to promote cell proliferation (Fan et al., 2018). This coating can avoid bacterial contamination in different fields, such as wounded skin, disinfection of biomedical implants and devices. Xie et al. adjusted the bandgap of the light-sensitive polydopamine/Ag3PO4/GO coating by changing the amount of GO in the hybrid coating, thereby increasing the utilization of visible light and achieving rapid sterilization of implants (Xie et al., 2018). Tominaga et al. study silver nanocluster (AgNC)-based antibacterial photocurable acrylic resin coatings prepared by incorporating the conjugate of dihydrolipoic acid-capped AgNC with tetraoctylammonium (TOA) (TOA-AgNC) (Tominaga et al., 2018). This coating can be used in healthcare/medical equipment, hospital infection prevention, and implantable equipment.

Furthermore, new coronavirus disease (Covid-19) has hit the world badly in 2019 and the effects still continue as of today. Thus, there are also some emerging investigation on nanomaterial-based strategies for antiviral therapies (Jackman et al., 2021), nanoparticle vaccines (Jackman et al., 2021), virus recognition at picomolar range (Cumbo et al., 2013), nanodiagnostics and nanotherapeutics (Derakhshan et al., 2021), etc. Because nanoscale functional materials as a kind of viral replication inhibitors are ideally suited to interact with virus particles.

In summary, the development in advanced/emerging technologies based on nanomaterial disinfection and sterilization technique (NDST) in life sciences and healthcare have been systematically reviewed. The findings are listed as follows:
(1)
The NDST has promising potential in water disinfection (e.g., drinking water safety, outdoor emergency water use in the army), air purification (e.g., indoor space), and medical healthcare (e.g., surgical wound). It has many advantages depending on nanomaterial types/designs, namely (a) no DBPs and no chemical consumption compared to traditional sterilization, (b) a sustainably green technology driven by absorbing solar light without high energy consumption compared to UV radiation, (c) easy recovery by magnetic separation, (d) non-metal nanomaterials without toxic metal leaching.
(2)
Four typical functional materials suggested in future work and their promising application in disinfection and sterilization are reviewed associated with a brief of synthesis and design. Metal nanoparticles regarding to AgNPs have been already used in life, medical, and military areas, this is a bacterial agent in the aspect of bactericidal performance and practical application is unsurpassed so far. Alternative disinfectors, namely oxides and sulfides particularly metal-metal coupling, have many salient features such as being highly hetero(photo)-catalytic (ROS formed in HO, O2
–, and 1O2), ferromagnetic, two-dimensional and so on. And, the new generation types (i.e., C-based and N-based nanomaterials) enable the exploitation of NDST without the metal leaching concern.
(3)
Several feasible devices based on nanomaterials, such as electrode sterilization, disinfection filter and so on, are also recommended and proposed. In contrast, some of practical issues such as the AgNPs agglomeration, noble metal with quick release, doped metal leaching, and spectral response in the lower wavelength need to be addressed prior to application in a larger scale study.
(4)
It is suggested that future studies can be extended to investigate (a) non-noble metal disinfectors, (b) multi-functional nanomaterials involving visible light response, (c) innovated multi-component nanocomposite/nanoheterojunction, and (d) hybrid techniques for disinfection and sterilization systems.

Overall, this review paper gives a comprehensive update on the development of nanomaterial disinfection and sterilization in the past ~30 years. The study provides insights into the advantages and disadvantages of the system and technologies of NDST, and suggests possible solutions for future studies.
